lesson_title: Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: Return std::vector from a function by {{c1::value}}. Modern C++ uses {{c2::move semantics}} to make this {{c3::efficient}}.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: {{c1::Move semantics}} allows transferring ownership of resources from one object to another, avoiding expensive {{c2::copies}}.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: An {{c1::rvalue}} is a {{c2::temporary}} object or value that doesn't persist beyond the expression. Examples: {{c3::literals}}, temporary objects, function return values.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: An {{c1::lvalue}} is an object with {{c2::persistent}} storage that can appear on the {{c3::left side}} of an assignment. Examples: variables, dereferenced pointers, references.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.concept: How move semantics optimize returning std::vector
cards.explanation:
When returning a vector by value:
  • Without move: entire vector copied (slow)
  • With move: only internal pointer transferred (fast)
  • Original vector left in valid but empty state
  • Compiler automatically uses move when returning local objects
This makes returning by value efficient.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: The {{c1::std::move()}} function converts an lvalue to an {{c2::rvalue reference}}, enabling move semantics.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.question: What happens after this code?
cards.explanation: std::move(v1) transfers ownership of v1's contents to v2. v1 is left in a valid but empty state (size = 0).
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.front: Should you use std::move() when returning a local vector from a function?
cards.back: No! The compiler automatically moves when returning local objects. Using std::move() can actually prevent optimizations like copy elision.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: {{c1::Copy elision}} (or RVO - Return Value Optimization) is a compiler optimization that eliminates unnecessary {{c2::copy or move}} operations when returning objects.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.text: After being moved from, an object is in a {{c1::valid but unspecified}} state. It can be {{c2::destroyed or assigned to}}, but don't rely on its {{c3::contents}}.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: Unnecessary and potentially harmful. Return v; is better - compiler automatically moves and can apply copy elision.
cards.explanation: Explicit std::move() prevents copy elision optimization. The compiler knows to move when returning local variables.
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics

cards.back: Return Value Optimization - compiler optimization that eliminates copy/move when returning objects
cards.source: 16.5 - Returning std::vector, and an introduction to move semantics
